title: OpenAI Trial: The 5 Most Surprising Moments

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Well Casey, the big trial between Elon Musk and OpenAI has been going on now for a couple weeks. It is nearing its end, and I thought we should do a breakdown of our top five juiciest drama moments from the trial so far.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Let's get into it.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Number five. Sam Altman testified on Tuesday of this week about what he called a hair-raising chat with Elon Musk. And in this chat, Altman asked Elon, 'If you have control over OpenAI, what happens when you die?' Musk reportedly responded, 'I haven't thought about it a ton, but maybe I should pass it to my children.'

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: All 14 of them? That's going to be quite a board of directors this company has. You know what I love about this particular story is that I had seen some chatter earlier in the trial that, you know, like the real reason that Elon Musk is pushing this is like he knows he's not going to win, but he just wants to embarrass Sam. Uh, I don't know what could be more embarrassing for Elon than the news that he was trying to turn OpenAI into a hereditary monarchy.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: New season of Succession just dropped. It's kids with unpronounceable names vying for control of OpenAI.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Alright, number four. This one was juicy. Helen Toner, former OpenAI board member, dragging Mira Murati. This was during the blip, the coup of Sam Altman that saw him fired and then rehired. In her recorded deposition, Toner essentially accused Mira Murati, who was at the time the company's chief technology officer, of being either dense or insincere, as she described Murati as strikingly unsupportive, remarkably passive, and said, quote, 'she seemed totally uninterested in telling her team that her conversations with us had been a significant factor in our decision to fire Sam.'

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so basically Mira, you know, according to the testimony, had been sort of, uh, quietly supplying the board with a lot of information about Sam Altman that the board then used in its decision to fire him, and agreed, Mira did, to become the company's interim CEO. But then when the whole thing happened, Mira was just sort of like very quiet and was like, 'I, you know, I don't know what's going on,' and then eventually, you know, basically said that she opposed Sam's removal, and that led to a great quote from Helen when she said, quote, 'she was waiting to see which way the wind would blow and she didn't realize that she was the wind.'

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Great quote. Put it on a bumper sticker. Alright, number three. This is the immortal diary of Greg Brockman. A thing of nightmares is to have your private journal entries read aloud in a courtroom. That happened during this trial to OpenAI president and co-founder Greg Brockman, who was required to submit personal journal entries he maintained during the early years of OpenAI as exhibits in the trial. The entries included Brockman's thoughts about his own ambition and his frustrations with Elon Musk. Uh, you can read many excerpts of this online, uh, but he is talking about how he wants to succeed at building the AGI, he wants to get to a billion dollars, uh, I presumably personal net worth.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: And that he wants to be respected for quote, "what I do." Relatable King. You know, I- I have to say, it made me uncomfortable to find out that this poor man's journal had been, like, dragged into court. Like, you know, I also keep a journal and, like, the thought that, you know, Elon Musk would be quizzing me about it on the witness stand someday sends a shiver down my spine.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Dear Musk legal team, please sue Casey. I desperately need to see what he says in his journal about me.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Now let me ask you this. So I assume that Greg thinks of his journal as a journal, but Elon Musk's lawyers and some press coverage has repeatedly called it a diary. Are they just doing that to embarrass Greg because diary is girl-coded?

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Because I don't like that. It's a journal.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Yeah, it's a journal. I mean, the bizarre part to me is that you would just keep a journal in a discoverable, like, place like Apple Notes or a Google Doc.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Well, what's a non-discoverable place to keep it?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: A bound book with a lock on it like you had when you were a pre-teen.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: And that just wouldn't be subject to discovery because it was written down instead of stored in the cloud?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: You have to burn it after reading. That's on- on page one, you know.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: All right. Here's Kevin Roose advocating for the destruction of evidence at trial.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Number two. This is the famous jackass trophy. Unfortunately, we have not seen imagery of this, but this was a trophy that was presented to an OpenAI employee early in the company's history after Elon Musk called him a jackass during a meeting. This trophy was presented to him by a plucky young safety executive named Dario Amodei, who would recur on future seasons of the show. And the trophy shows the back half of a donkey and says on it, "Never stop being a jackass for safety."

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: My question is, as enjoyable as that story is, Kevin, why is this evidence at a trial? What is this supposed to be evidence of?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: I think they're trying to introduce it on the OpenAI side to sort of gesture at the fact that Elon Musk did not seem to care about safety as much as he claimed.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: But they're like, "Look, you can tell he didn't care about safety. Look at this jackass trophy that we made."

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: I don't know if that's really adding up for me.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Yeah, it doesn't track exactly for me either. This feels like a legal strategy supplied by ChatGPT.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Bring out the jackass trophy. Where's that trophy?

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: I- I mostly think it's very funny and it goes to one of my other sort of conspiratorial beliefs about this trial, which is that it's just a way to smuggle a bunch of gossip into the public record.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: And we support that aspect.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: We support that. It has been a true gift to journalists and historians.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: All right. Number one. The spiciest moment, or at least my favorite moment from the trial so far, was the testimony of Ilya Sutskever, former chief scientist and co-founder at OpenAI. A very quotable man who sort of talks in riddles.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: and-and parables. And he had a very memorable answer to a question from the judge in the case about the difference between the AI systems of a decade ago and the AI systems of today. He said it was like the difference between an ant and a cat. He also responded to a question about OpenAI's computing needs by saying that OpenAI needed more funding to build what he called quote "a big computer."

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: And I just think that's a beautiful phrase. I like big computer.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: I believe his exact quote was "if there's no funding, there is no big computer." Yes. And he was really determined for there to be a big computer.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: And now we have it.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Yep. Well, Casey, any other notes that I missed from the trial so far?

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: My other favorite moment of the trial concerned the testimony of Shivon Zilis, who is a longtime employee of Musk and mother to four of his children, who also served on the OpenAI board. And during this time became a kind of covert liaison between like Musk and the OpenAI folks until it came out that she was the mother of his children and the OpenAI was like, "um, you have to leave the board now." But like, that is a true like Hollywood twist.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Yeah, she was the mole for Elon inside OpenAI who was like ferreting him information and is no longer on the OpenAI board for reasons that I think are probably understandable.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Quite understandable.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Yes. The thing that I found most interesting from this trial is that everyone knew that this technology was going to do exactly what it did. In like 2017, everyone was like, "this is going to become the most important technology in the world, there'll be huge fights over who governs it and whether they become a global AGI dictator." And they were putting this stuff all in writing. This was before any of this shit worked. It was like when AI could still basically barely complete a sentence, they were talking about how this would totally reshape global governance and politics and the economy. And they were completely right about all of that, even though they all ended up hating each other.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: I think it, I mean, it is very interesting how much they were right about. And I think it probably speaks to the fact that they wrote it down that even they on some level had some difficulty believing that it would be true, you know what I mean? Like it all did still very much feel like sci-fi 10 years ago.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: They felt like they were playing a board game.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: They were like, did they though? I mean, because they really did believe it. Or I think like most of the players involved really did believe it. So like I don't think it was a board game to them exactly, but it was all just in the realm of speculation.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: It also strikes me that this might be like the last trial where like emails and written records play a big role. Like I feel like the companies and the people at the top of these companies have learned in the past 10 years like not to put anything in writing. And so they do everything on like disappearing signals or

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: ...whatever, and so this may be the last great deposit of receipts that we have in this world.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Yeah, companies are getting much better at destroying evidence.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Can't destroy evidence if it never existed.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: And there you have it, those are our top moments from the Elon Musk OpenAI trial. We look forward to many more trials.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Every day with you is a trial.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: So I hear.

highlights: Ilya Sutskever compared the evolution of AI systems to the difference between an ant and a cat, emphasizing that the development of AGI is entirely dependent on securing massive funding for "a big computer.".

The trial revealed that OpenAI's founders accurately predicted in 2017 that AI would become the world's most important technology and reshape global governance, long before the systems were actually functional.
Testimony regarding Elon Musk suggested he once proposed passing control of OpenAI to his children, leading to accusations that he intended to turn the non-profit into a hereditary monarchy.

Internal depositions highlighted deep cultural friction within the company, including claims that leadership figures were "waiting to see which way the wind would blow" during Sam Altman’s brief firing.

Analysts noted that this trial serves as a rare historical "deposit of receipts," as tech leaders are increasingly moving away from discoverable written records like emails and journals in favor of encrypted messaging.
summary:
In this transcript, Kevin Roose and Casey Newton review the most significant and dramatic moments from the ongoing legal battle between Elon Musk and OpenAI. The discussion highlights a series of "juicy" revelations brought to light through depositions and evidence, including Sam Altman’s testimony that Musk once suggested passing control of OpenAI to his children. Other key points include the friction between former board member Helen Toner and CTO Mira Murati during Altman's brief firing, the forced public reading of President Greg Brockman’s private journals, and the anecdotal "Jackass Trophy" used by OpenAI to challenge Musk’s self-proclaimed focus on AI safety.

Beyond the courtroom gossip, the hosts explore deeper themes regarding the foresight and ambition of OpenAI’s founders. They note that as early as 2017, the leadership correctly predicted that AI would become a world-altering technology, leading to intense power struggles over its governance. The conversation concludes with the observation that this trial may represent one of the last "great deposits of receipts" in the tech industry, as executives increasingly move toward disappearing messaging platforms to avoid leaving a discoverable paper trail of their internal conflicts and strategic maneuvers.
description:
As the ongoing legal feud between Elon Musk and Sam Altman heats up, we break down five key moments that have stood out to us (so far) from the landmark A.I. trial.

Hard Fork is a weekly look into the future that's already here. Hosts Kevin Roose and Casey Newton explore stories from the bleeding edge of tech.

Casey’s publication, Platformer: https://www.platformer.news/

FOLLOW US ON SOCIAL
TikTok: https://www.tiktok.com/@hardfork
Instagram:
Kevin Roose - https://www.instagram.com/kevinroose/
Casey Newton - https://www.instagram.com/crumbler/

Twitter (X):
Kevin - https://x.com/kevinroose
Casey - https://x.com/CaseyNewton

Threads:

Casey - https://www.threads.net/@crumbler

Subscribe to the podcast:

Spotify - https://open.spotify.com/show/44fllCS2FTFr2x2kjP9xeT?si=86ba626802b849cd
Amazon - https://music.amazon.com/podcasts/7c7fe198-e6a8-41a8-b0fe-1d46b976dcd8/hard-fork
YouTube Music - https://music.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLB9gMmtMLXxsa8C0PzHFL2tJFh7FrKrYD&si=Fwb9Uwon1ZneNsBp
The New York Times - https://www.nytimes.com/column/hard-fork

“Hard Fork” is hosted by Kevin Roose and Casey Newton. Produced by Rachel Cohn and Whitney Jones. We’re edited by Vjeran Pavic. 
Engineering by Chris Wood and original music by Dan Powell, Marion Lozano, Sophia Lanman and Rowan Niemisto. Fact-checking by Caitlin Love. Video production by Jake Nicol, Chris Schodt and Sawyer Roque. Podcast Art Direction by Benjamin Wilkerson Tousley. Podcast Art & Animations by Julian Hespenheide. Additional Motion graphics by Phil Robibero. Thumbnails by Julia Moburg and Tara Godvin. Special thanks to Paula Szuchman, Pui-Wing Tam and Dahlia Haddad.
topics: Highlights from the Elon Musk vs. OpenAI legal trial
Internal drama and board conflicts within OpenAI
The role of personal journals and artifacts as evidence in court
Reflections on early AI safety and governance predictions
The evolution of corporate record-keeping and data discovery
